preamble: The transformation from an alveolar nasal to an alveolar flap is a type of liquidization or rhotacism, where a nasal consonant loses its nasal airflow and becomes a rhotic liquid, typically as an allophonic variation in specific phonetic environments.

examples.note: In rapid speech, the alveolar nasal can assimilate into a following rhotic, becoming a nasalized flap or a standard flap.
Nasal flapping where the nasal-stop cluster reduces to a nasalized alveolar flap, which is functionally equivalent to the rhotic flap in terms of duration.

sources: Vance, T. J. (2008). The Sounds of Japanese.
Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
